title: The Quiet Revolution in City Buses
author: Fictional Author
text:
For most of the twentieth century, the city bus was treated as the ugly stepchild of urban transportation. Rail got the romance. Cars got the money. Buses got whatever was left. That has been changing quietly, unevenly, and in ways that are worth paying attention to.

The shift began with a small observation made by planners in a handful of mid-sized cities about fifteen years ago. The data kept showing the same pattern. When a bus route got a dedicated lane, ridership jumped. When the stops were consolidated and raised to platform height, boarding times collapsed. When riders could pay at the curb instead of at the farebox, dwell times at each stop shrank by ten, twenty, sometimes thirty seconds. These were not huge interventions. They were the kind of thing any transit agency could do with a modest capital budget. But stacked together they turned a bus route into something that behaved, from a rider's perspective, more like a subway line than like a creaky old local bus.

The name for this bundle of interventions is bus rapid transit, or BRT. The phrase had floated around since the 1970s, mostly in academic papers and in a few South American cities where rail was simply out of reach. It took a long time for the idea to find real purchase in North America. Part of the reason is cultural. A bus is still, in many American imaginations, a vehicle for people who cannot afford a car. Rail has a middle-class patina. Light rail projects get festive ribbon-cuttings attended by mayors. A restriped bus lane gets a press release.

Another part of the reason is institutional. Transit agencies are not usually in charge of the streets their buses run on. Streets belong to the city transportation department, or to the state department of transportation, or to some hybrid body that has to negotiate with both. Giving a bus its own lane means taking that lane away from cars, or from parking, and every one of those decisions triggers a political fight. Rail is easier, in a weird way, because a train on a separated track doesn't need to pick a fight with anyone over the marginal foot of asphalt.

But the arithmetic has gotten harder to ignore. A good BRT corridor costs between five and twenty million dollars per mile. A light rail line in the same corridor costs between one hundred and three hundred million per mile. The ridership numbers, in corridor after corridor, come out within spitting distance of each other when the buses are done well. Not always. Rail still beats buses on the highest-volume trunk routes in the densest cities, where a six-car light rail train can clear a platform that a 60-foot articulated bus cannot. But for the vast majority of American urban corridors, the bus math works.

The quiet revolution has been, in effect, a slow surrender to that math. You see it in cities that are not usually grouped with the transit heroes. Minneapolis, which opened its first aBRT line in 2016. Albuquerque, whose ART line along Central Avenue reshaped a corridor that had been declining for decades. Indianapolis, which built an entire grid of high-frequency lines branded as the Red, Purple, and Blue Lines. Richmond, Virginia, whose Pulse line has become a minor case study in what a modest project can do when it is executed with discipline.

What makes a BRT corridor actually work, though, is rarely the thing civic leaders talk about in the press release. The press release talks about new buses. Sometimes electric ones. Always with nicer seats and USB chargers. These are real improvements and riders notice them, but they are not what moves the ridership numbers. The thing that moves the ridership numbers is frequency. A bus that comes every ten minutes, all day, is a bus you can use without consulting a schedule. A bus that comes every thirty minutes is a bus you plan your day around, and planning your day around a bus is something most people will do once or twice and then give up.

Frequency is boring. It does not lend itself to ribbon-cuttings. It requires buying more buses and paying more drivers, year after year, forever. Capital money is plentiful in American transit funding. Operating money is scarce. This is the deepest structural reason that BRT projects, even when they are built, often underperform their promise. The physical infrastructure gets installed, the lane gets painted, the stations get built, and then the service plan calls for a bus every twenty minutes on weekdays and every thirty minutes on weekends, because the agency cannot afford more. A twenty-minute bus in its own lane is still a twenty-minute bus.

The cities that have made BRT actually work are the ones that figured out how to fund the operating side too. Albuquerque did it by consolidating local routes into the ART corridor, freeing up the operating hours that had been scattered across parallel coverage. Richmond did it through a dedicated regional transit sales tax. Minneapolis has been doing it by slowly accruing ridership and political support on each successive aBRT line, building a case that the next one deserves a real operating budget. None of these stories are heroic. They are patient, bureaucratic, and often contingent on a single county executive or mayor who simply decided to care.

There is a second quiet revolution running alongside the first, which is the electrification of urban bus fleets. The arithmetic here is still being worked out. An electric bus costs about twice as much to buy as a diesel one. Over a twelve-year service life, the lower fuel and maintenance costs close most of that gap, but the gap closes faster in a city with cheap electricity and a warm climate, and closes slower in a cold climate with expensive power. The first generation of electric buses also disappointed operators in ways that are now being ironed out. Cold weather range was worse than advertised. Battery replacements came earlier than projected. Charging infrastructure was harder to integrate into existing bus garages than the sales pitches implied.

By the middle of this decade, most of those issues are being addressed, and the economic case is getting simpler. Several large agencies, including Los Angeles Metro and Chicago's CTA, have made public commitments to a fully electric fleet by the mid-2030s. Whether they will hit those targets is an open question. The buses are not the binding constraint any more. The binding constraints are the electrical capacity at the bus yards, the ability to schedule around charging times, and the ability of state grids to absorb the new load during the overnight charging window.

An observation worth sitting with: electrification and BRT are often treated as two stories, but in the communities that are doing both, they are one story. A new BRT corridor is the natural place to introduce a new bus. The fleet that runs the corridor is small and self-contained. The charging infrastructure can be concentrated at one or two yards. The political coalition that supports BRT is also, usually, the political coalition that supports electrification. When these two initiatives are joined up, the combined project sells much better than either one alone. The corridor becomes visibly modern in a way that feels coherent.

When they are not joined up, both suffer. A beautiful new corridor running old diesel buses feels like a half-measure. A shiny new electric bus stuck in traffic in a mixed-flow lane tells riders that the agency cared more about the photo opportunity than about their commute. The combined project is greater than the sum of its parts precisely because the two components reinforce each other's legibility to the riding public.

A third thread in this story is harder to name but may be the most important. For a long time, American transit planning was organized around the peak hour commute. You built service to move office workers from inner-ring suburbs to downtown job centers in the morning, and back in the evening. The rest of the day, and the rest of the city, got whatever you had left. The pandemic scrambled that model. Downtown office occupancy in most large American cities is still well below its pre-pandemic peak, and even in the cities where it has mostly recovered, the Tuesday-Wednesday-Thursday pattern of hybrid work has made peak service hard to justify the way it used to be.

What the pandemic also revealed, surprisingly, was that midday and evening ridership on many urban bus routes was far more resilient than the commuter ridership was. The people riding the bus at one in the afternoon on a Tuesday turned out to be doing errands, getting to medical appointments, going to work in industries that never went remote, and making the kind of stitched-together trips that the old peak-hour model had undercounted for decades. These riders had been there all along. They were simply invisible in a planning culture organized around the white-collar commute.

A handful of agencies have started to redesign their networks around this insight. They are flattening the peaks, boosting midday frequency, and letting the commuter peaks thin out. They are treating the bus network as a general-purpose urban utility rather than as a rush-hour relief valve for highway congestion. This is a profound reorientation, and it is happening without fanfare in city after city. Houston did a version of it before the pandemic, with its 2015 system redesign. Columbus did another version after. Dallas is in the middle of one now. Each of these redesigns is locally contested, locally compromised, and locally imperfect, but in aggregate they point somewhere.

The somewhere they point is toward a bus network that looks less like a commuter service and more like a circulatory system. Every major corridor runs frequent service all day. The connections between corridors are clean enough that a rider can make a one-transfer trip without consulting an app. The network is legible in the way a grid of streets is legible, rather than the way a timetable is legible.

If this sounds boring, that is because it is boring. It is also expensive, slow, and politically difficult. What it is not, anymore, is hypothetical. You can ride this kind of network in a few American cities right now. You can ride a version of it in Mexico City, and a more advanced version in Bogotá, where the TransMilenio system has been doing this for over two decades. You can ride a radically more advanced version in Curitiba, which invented modern BRT in the first place in the 1970s and has spent fifty years refining it. The technology is not exotic. The techniques are well understood. The thing that has been in short supply, in the North American context, is patience and institutional coherence.

A reader who has followed transit coverage for a long time may find this account insufficiently exciting. Where is the hyperloop? Where are the autonomous shuttles? Where is the mention of driverless micro-transit networks summoned by an app? These technologies exist, in demonstration form, and they occupy an enormous share of the transit press. They have done relatively little for the actual rider. The rider's experience is overwhelmingly determined by the frequency, reliability, and comfort of the bus that is running today. Micro-transit helps at the margins of low-density neighborhoods. Autonomy may, eventually, make drivers easier to hire. Neither is going to reshape the rider's experience in the next decade. The bus, in its dedicated lane, coming every ten minutes, is what will. The quiet revolution is quiet precisely because there is nothing glamorous about it. The people doing the work are running procurements, negotiating with public works departments, persuading city councils to paint red lanes on existing streets, and hiring drivers. This is how transit actually improves. It happens at the pace of municipal budget cycles. It happens one corridor at a time. It happens when somebody on staff is obstinate enough to keep pushing through five years of meetings to get twelve miles of red paint on a street. When it works, it does not feel like a revolution to anyone involved. It feels like a slog that eventually started to pay off.

That may be the most useful thing to remember the next time a glossy transportation technology is announced with a giant press event and a CGI render. The actual future of urban transportation, in most places, will look a lot like the present, done patiently, slightly better each year, with red lanes and frequent buses and a slowly greening fleet. The revolution is already here. It just does not look like one.

One more thread before closing. The labor story of urban transit is undergoing its own quiet change, and it is entangled with everything else described here. Since roughly 2019, transit agencies across the United States have struggled to hire and retain bus operators. The shortage has been blamed on wages, on working conditions, on post-pandemic attrition, on competition from delivery and ride-hail driving. All of these are real. The underlying issue is that driving a city bus is a hard job, and for a long time it was not paid or respected like the hard job it is. An operator has to navigate a 40-foot vehicle through dense urban traffic, manage a fare system, answer passenger questions, de-escalate conflicts, make a schedule, and do it all for an eight-hour shift that often starts before dawn or ends after midnight. The agencies that have solved their hiring problems have done it by raising starting wages substantially, offering real career ladders, investing in operator facilities, and taking assaults on operators seriously as a safety and policy issue rather than as an occupational hazard to be absorbed.

The operator shortage has ironic consequences for the service-expansion ambitions described above. A city can buy all the buses it wants, paint all the lanes, build all the stations, and still be unable to run the service plan it designed, because there are not enough people in the seat. Every agency leader in the country now spends more time on recruiting and retention than their predecessors did a decade ago. This is the unglamorous, humanistic core of the work. If the revolution is quiet, the people driving it, in every literal sense, are the ones who need to be paid properly and treated well for any of the rest of it to matter.

A final caveat. Nothing in the story above implies that buses are a universal solution. There are dense trunk corridors where rail is the right answer and buses cannot substitute. There are low-density outer neighborhoods where fixed-route service of any kind is the wrong tool, and flexible services, whether on-demand vans or subsidized taxi programs, serve riders better. The case for buses is not a case against rail or against innovation. It is a case against the habit of letting the most visible and most expensive option crowd out the less visible option that would move more people, sooner, at lower cost. Transit networks work as networks. A rail backbone is stronger when a good bus grid feeds it. A bus grid is stronger when a rail backbone takes the highest-volume corridors off its hands. The quiet revolution is about taking the bus half of that partnership seriously after decades of treating it as an afterthought.
